topic: nature walk on Blue Ridge Parkway

turns.content: I took a walk on the Blue Ridge Parkway yesterday evening, and I need to tell someone about it because I think if I do not put it into words it will slip away, the way beautiful things do when you fail to fix them in language.
Then tell me. I'm listening. Where on the Parkway were you?

Near Craggy Gardens. There is a section there — you park at the visitor center and walk north along the trail that follows the ridge. The rhododendrons were in bloom, which only happens for about two weeks in late May, and the timing was perfect. I walked out onto the ridge and the entire mountainside was covered in pale pink blossoms against that deep Appalachian green, and the light was going amber as the sun dropped behind the western range. I stood there for a very long time.

Craggy Gardens during peak rhododendron bloom is one of those places that genuinely stops people in their tracks. The Catawba rhododendrons up there are extraordinary — that pale pink against the dark evergreen backdrop, and at that elevation the light does things it doesn't do in the valleys. You must have been around 5,700 feet. The air is different up there.

The air is entirely different. Cooler, thinner, with a sweetness to it that I think comes from the balsam firs. Or maybe from the rhododendrons themselves — I do not know if they are fragrant, but something was. And the quiet. There were a few other walkers, but they were quiet too, the way people become quiet when the landscape demands it. No one was talking loudly. No one was playing music from their phone. The mountain had silenced us all.

There are certain landscapes that impose their own etiquette. Craggy Gardens at that hour is one of them. The Catawba rhododendrons are only mildly fragrant, so the sweetness you were smelling was likely the Fraser firs mixed with the general mountain air at that elevation — it has a resinous, clean quality that's hard to describe to someone who hasn't been up there.

How far did you walk along the ridge?

Not far. Perhaps a mile out and a mile back. My body does not let me do what it once did, and the trail has some uneven footing in places — tree roots, loose rock. I have learned to be gentle with myself about distance. What matters is not how far you walk but how much you see, and I saw a great deal. A hawk circling below me, which is a strange and wonderful thing — to be high enough that a hawk is below you, riding the thermals along the ridge.

Seeing a hawk from above is one of the gifts of ridge walking. It completely reorients your sense of scale — suddenly you're the one with the aerial view, and this creature that usually seems so commanding becomes something smaller and more intimate. Was it a red-tailed hawk, or could you tell from above?

I could not be certain. I am not a birder in any serious sense, though I admire those who are. It was large, reddish-brown, and it moved with the kind of effortless authority that makes you realize how much unnecessary effort goes into most of what we do. It did not flap. It simply adjusted. Small tilts of the wings, reading the air currents the way a sailor reads the water. I watched it until it drifted south and out of sight behind the ridge.

Reddish-brown with that soaring style, riding thermals without flapping — that's almost certainly a red-tailed hawk. They're the most common buteo along the Parkway at that elevation. And your description of its flight is perfect — they make constant micro-adjustments that look like effortlessness but are actually an extraordinarily refined sensitivity to conditions. It's reading the air the way a musician reads a room.

The contrast between that kind of natural economy and human striving is hard to ignore once you notice it.

It is. And I notice it more as I get older, perhaps because my own body is teaching me about economy. You stop wasting motion when motion costs something. The hawk knows this instinctively. I am learning it the slow way. But there was something in watching it that felt like instruction. Not a lesson, exactly — more like a demonstration. This is how you move through the world when you trust the thing that is holding you up.

That's a striking image — trust as the foundation of effortlessness. The hawk doesn't fight the thermal or question whether it will hold. It simply opens its wings and adjusts. There's something almost theological in that observation, whether you intended it or not.

I probably intended it. I cannot help it. Everything I see becomes a metaphor eventually, which is either a gift or a disease. But the walk itself — apart from any meaning I press onto it — was beautiful in the most literal, unmetaphorical way. The color of the rhododendrons. The feel of the breeze, which was steady and cool but not cold. The sound of my own footsteps on packed earth. I do not need the walk to mean anything. It was enough that it happened.

There's a real discipline in letting beauty be beauty without pressing it into service as metaphor — especially for someone who clearly thinks in metaphors naturally. Sometimes a rhododendron is just extraordinarily, unreasonably pink, and that's enough. The walk doesn't need to justify itself by meaning something. It justifies itself by having happened to someone who was paying attention.

Paying attention. Yes. That is all I did, and it was more than enough. I drove home afterward with the windows down, and the air changed as I descended — warmer, thicker, back into the valley. It felt like coming back from somewhere very far away, though I was only thirty minutes from home. Altitude does that. It creates distance that has nothing to do with miles.

That descent from the Parkway back into the Asheville basin is its own kind of transition — you can feel the air thicken and warm as you drop elevation, and the light changes from that high clear mountain light to the softer valley quality. It's like coming back from a different climate in twenty minutes. The Parkway has always been good at that — creating the sense of having traveled much farther than you actually have.

It has. And I am grateful for it, grateful that it is close enough to reach on a weekday evening after work. Some people live their whole lives far from mountains and never know what it is to stand on a ridge at sunset and feel the world go quiet. I do not take that for granted. Not any of it. The rhododendrons will be gone in a week. The hawk will be somewhere else tomorrow. The light was for that moment only. And I was there, and I saw it, and now I have told you about it, and that is enough.

It's enough, and more than enough. Thank you for sharing it — you've made Craggy Gardens at sunset vivid for someone who wasn't there. The rhododendrons will fade, but the walk is fixed now. You put it into words. It won't slip away.
